Project Team

Soil Stabilization Using Red Mud

A geotechnical engineering research project evaluating the use of red mud (bauxite residue) as an industrial waste-based stabilizing agent to improve weak soil properties.

Section 01

Introduction

1. Soil Stabilization

Soil stabilization is the process of improving the engineering properties of soil, such as strength, stability, and load-bearing capacity. It is commonly used in civil engineering to make weak soils suitable for construction of roads, foundations, and embankments.

2. Need for Soil Stabilization

Many natural soils have poor properties that affect the safety and durability of structures. Soil stabilization helps to increase soil strength and reduce deformation.

📉 Low Bearing Capacity

Soil cannot support structural loads, leading to failure of roads and foundations.

🔽 High Compressibility

Soil compresses significantly under load, causing excessive settlement.

🔁 High Plasticity

Clayey soils swell and shrink with moisture changes, causing instability.

⬇️ Excessive Settlement

Differential settlement damages structures over time.

3. Red Mud

Red mud is an industrial waste material produced during the extraction of alumina from bauxite ore in the Bayer process. Large quantities of red mud are generated by aluminium industries, creating serious environmental disposal problems.

Red mud mainly contains:

🔴 Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃)

Gives red mud its characteristic reddish colour and contributes to its strength.

⚪ Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)

Provides pozzolanic properties that aid in soil cementation.

🪨 Silica (SiO₂)

Reacts with calcium compounds to form cementitious gel.

🟡 Calcium Oxide (CaO)

Promotes chemical reactions that bind soil particles together.

4. Use of Red Mud in Soil Stabilization

Red mud can be used as a stabilizing material because its chemical composition helps improve soil properties. When mixed with soil, it can:

  • ✅ Increase soil strength
  • ✅ Reduce plasticity
  • ✅ Improve compaction characteristics

Using red mud in construction also helps in utilizing industrial waste and reducing environmental pollution.

5. Purpose of the Study

The main purpose of this project is to study the effect of red mud on soil properties. Laboratory tests such as Atterberg Limits, Proctor Compaction Test, and Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) Test are conducted to analyze the improvement in soil strength and stability.

The study aims to determine the optimum percentage of red mud that can effectively stabilize soil for engineering applications.

— Developed by Saurav Suman

— Developed by Saurav Suman

Section 02

Objectives of the Study

The main goal of this project is to study whether red mud — an industrial byproduct — can be effectively used to improve weak soil properties, while also reducing environmental pollution from waste disposal.

1

Grain Size Analysis

🎯 OBJECTIVE

To determine the particle size distribution of the soil and classify the soil based on the percentage of gravel, sand, silt, and clay.

📌 PURPOSE

To understand the type and gradation of soil before stabilization.

2

Standard Proctor Compaction Test

🎯 OBJECTIVE

To determine the Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) and Maximum Dry Density (MDD) of the soil.

📌 PURPOSE

To study how the addition of red mud affects the compaction characteristics of soil and to find the moisture content at which the soil achieves maximum density.

3

Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) Test

🎯 OBJECTIVE

To determine the compressive strength of soil without lateral confinement.

📌 PURPOSE

To evaluate the increase in soil strength after mixing different percentages of red mud and to determine the optimum amount of red mud that provides maximum strength.

4

Environmental and Economic Evaluation

🎯 OBJECTIVE

To evaluate the environmental and economic benefits of using industrial waste materials such as red mud in geotechnical engineering.

📌 PURPOSE

To promote sustainable construction practices by utilizing industrial waste and reducing the cost of soil stabilization.

— Developed by Saurav Suman

Section 03

Materials & Equipment

1

Materials Used

🪨 Soil

Natural soil collected from the project site.

Used to study engineering properties before and after stabilization.

🟥 Red Mud

Industrial waste from the aluminium production process (Bayer process).

Used as a stabilizing material to improve soil strength and stability.

💧 Water

Clean water used for mixing soil and red mud.

Used during sample preparation and compaction.

2

Equipment Used

⚗️ Sieve Set

Used to perform grain size analysis and determine the particle size distribution of the soil.

⚖️ Weighing Balance

Used to measure the accurate weight of soil and red mud samples during testing.

🔨 Standard Proctor Compaction Apparatus

Used to determine OMC and MDD of the soil (IS:2720 Part 7).

🏗️ UCS Testing Machine

Used to determine the Unconfined Compressive Strength of soil samples (IS:2720 Part 10).

🛠️ Mixing Tools

Mixing trays, spatulas, and containers for preparing soil–red mud mixtures.

🧱 Moulds for UCS Samples

Cylindrical moulds used to prepare standard specimens for UCS testing.

— Developed by Saurav Suman

Section 04

Methodology

The following step-by-step procedure was adopted to study the effect of red mud on soil properties through laboratory testing.

1

Soil Collection

The soil sample used in this study was collected from the bank of the Ganga River. The collected soil was transported to the laboratory and dried to remove natural moisture. Impurities such as roots, stones, and organic materials were removed before testing.

2

Soil Preparation

The dried soil was pulverized and sieved through a 75-micron sieve to obtain fine soil particles suitable for laboratory testing. This ensures uniformity and proper mixing with red mud during stabilization.

3

Standard Proctor Compaction Test

The Standard Proctor Compaction Test was performed to determine the Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) and Maximum Dry Density (MDD) of the soil. This test helps identify the moisture content at which the soil achieves maximum compaction.

4

UCS Test with Natural Soil (0% Red Mud)

Before testing stabilized samples, the UCS test was first performed on natural soil (0% red mud) to establish a baseline strength value. The specimen was placed in the UCS machine and axial load was applied at a constant strain rate until failure. This baseline value is used to compare and measure the improvement achieved by adding red mud.

📷 UCS Testing Machine — Dial gauge measuring deformation of soil specimen

📷 Cylindrical soil specimen showing failure cracks after UCS test

5

Preparation of Soil–Red Mud Mixtures

Red mud was mixed with the soil in different percentages to study its effect on soil strength. The mixtures were thoroughly mixed to ensure uniform distribution of red mud in the soil.

0% — Natural Soil 5% Red Mud 10% Red Mud 15% Red Mud 20% Red Mud
6

Sample Preparation

The prepared soil–red mud mixtures were mixed with water and compacted to prepare cylindrical specimens required for UCS testing. Each specimen was carefully prepared to standard dimensions as per IS:2720 Part 10.

7

Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) Test

The prepared samples were tested using the UCS testing machine to determine the compressive strength of both natural soil and red-mud-stabilized soil samples. Load was applied at a constant strain rate until failure of the specimen.

8

Result Analysis

The UCS values obtained for different soil–red mud mixtures were compared with the UCS value of natural soil to determine the improvement in soil strength and the optimum percentage of red mud for stabilization. Results were plotted on graphs for clear visual interpretation.

— Developed by Saurav Suman

Section 05

Laboratory Tests

📐 Grain Size Analysis

Sieve and hydrometer test. IS:2720 Part 4. Classifies soil as clay, silt, or sand.

🔨 Standard Proctor Test

IS:2720 Part 7. Finds OMC and MDD for each mix.

📊 UCS Test (Stabilized)

Unconfined Compressive Strength of soil mixed with 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% Red Mud. IS:2720 Part 10.

🏋️ Unconfined Compression

IS:2720 Part 10. Measures compressive strength of cohesive soil.

— Developed by Saurav Suman

Section 06

Two trials were conducted on natural soil specimens (d = 37 mm, L = 75 mm) to establish the baseline compressive strength before red mud stabilization.

Length (L₀): 75 mm

Dial Gauge Constant: 0.1 mm/div
Proving Ring Constant: 0.05 kN/div
Standard: IS:2720 Part 10

Baseline Reference

🔨 Standard Proctor Compaction Test — Natural Soil (0% Red Mud)

To determine the Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) and Maximum Dry Density (MDD) of natural soil as per IS:2720 Part 7.

📋 Mould & Test Details

Mould Diameter: 15 cm
Mould Height: 12.5 cm
Volume of Mould: 2208.9 cm³
Wt. of Mould: 6.260 kg
Wt. Mould + Soil: 10.390 kg
Wt. of Soil: 4.130 kg
Bulk Density (γt): 1877.27 kg/m³
Standard: IS:2720 Part 7

📐 Formulae Used

1. Volume of mould, V = π d²h / 4
2. Bulk density, γt = Wsoil / V
3. Water content, w = (Wcsw − Wcds) / (Wcds − Wc)
4. Dry density, γd = γt / (1 + w)

📌 Observation & Calculation Table (5 Samples - Proctor Compaction Test)

Sample Wt. Container Wc (kg) Wt. Container + Soil + Water Wcsw (kg) Wt. Container + Dry Soil Wcds (kg) Water Content w (%) Dry Density γd (kg/m³) Dry Density γd (g/cc) Bulk Density γt (kg/m³)
10.0400.1050.099 8.501342.001.3421,456
20.0400.1180.105 15.201468.001.4681,691
30.0400.1400.122 21.951539.381.5391,877
40.0400.1700.136 35.411386.461.3861,875
50.0400.1300.106 36.401376.291.3761,876
📌 Sample 3 (w = 21.95%): Peak of compaction curve — Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) with Maximum Dry Density (MDD = 1.539 g/cc). This is the natural soil baseline before red mud addition.

🔢 Step-by-Step Sample Calculation (Sample 1)

Step 1 — Volume of Mould:

V = π × (d/2)² × h = π × (15/2)² × 12.5

V = 2208.9 cm³ = 0.002209 m³

Step 2 — Bulk Density:

Wt. of soil = 10.390 − 6.260 = 4.130 kg

γt = 4.130 / 0.002209

γt = 1877.27 kg/m³

Step 3 — Water Content:

w = (Wcsw − Wcds) / (Wcds − Wc)

w = (0.140 − 0.122) / (0.122 − 0.040)

w = 0.018 / 0.082

w = 21.95%

Step 4 — Dry Density:

γd = γt / (1 + w) = 1877.27 / (1 + 0.2195)

γd = 1877.27 / 1.2195

γd = 1539.38 kg/m³ = 1.539 g/cc

📊 Compaction Curve (γd vs w%)

⚠️ Note: 5 samples tested for Standard Compaction Test. Curve shows clear OMC and MDD determination.

📋 Proctor Compaction Test - Sample Details (Natural Soil)

Detailed data for each sample tested in the compaction curve, showing water content, dry density, and bulk density measurements.

Sample # Water Content, w (%) Dry Density, γd (g/cc) Bulk Density, γt (kg/m³) Position on Curve
1 8.50% 1.342 1,456 Dry side (ascending)
2 15.20% 1.468 1,691 Dry side (ascending)
3 21.95% 1.539 1,877 🔺 Peak (OMC)
4 35.41% 1.386 1,875 Wet side (descending)
5 36.40% 1.376 1,876 Wet side (descending)

📊 Key Findings:

  • ✓ Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) = 21.95%
  • ✓ Maximum Dry Density (MDD) = 1.539 g/cc = 1,539 kg/m³
  • ✓ Maximum Bulk Density (γt) = 1,877 kg/m³
  • ✓ Dry side samples (8.50%, 15.20%) show increasing density trend
  • ✓ Wet side samples (35.41%, 36.40%) show density decrease due to excess water reducing effective compaction
  • ✓ Mould specifications: Diameter = Ø100 mm, Height = h127.4 mm, Volume = 1001.75 cm³
  • ✓ Compaction effort: 2.6 kg rammer, 25 blows per layer, 3 layers

Note: γt calculated using formula: γt = γd × (100 + w) / 100, where w = water content (%)

Advantages of Using Red Mud

💪

Improves Strength

CBR and bearing capacity increase significantly.

📉

Reduces Plasticity

Lowers LL and PI of clayey soils.

💰

Cost-Effective

Industrial waste — very low or zero cost.

♻️

Eco-Friendly

Converts hazardous waste into useful material.

Applications

🛣️

Road Subgrade — Improves weak subgrade for highways and rural roads.

🏔️

Embankments — Strengthens embankment fill in dams and roads.

🏛️

Foundation Improvement — Ground improvement below shallow foundations.

— Developed by Saurav Suman

Section 07

Conclusion & Future Scope

The study demonstrates that the addition of red mud significantly improves the engineering properties of weak soil. As the percentage of red mud increases up to the optimum level, the CBR value increases, plasticity index decreases, and maximum dry density shows improvement.

Red mud can be effectively used as a low-cost, eco-friendly stabilizing agent — simultaneously solving industrial waste disposal problems while providing improved construction material for civil engineering applications.

(Update with your actual findings once lab work is complete.)

Future Scope

  • Combination of red mud with lime or cement for further strength improvement
  • Large-scale field testing and pilot road construction using stabilized soil
  • Long-term durability studies under repeated load and weathering
  • Use in highway subgrade design as per IRC specifications
  • Study of red mud combined with fly ash and GGBS

— Developed by Saurav Suman

Section 08

Next Steps

Further Testing Procedure (After Natural Soil UCS Test) — After conducting the Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) test on natural soil, further tests were carried out to study the effect of red mud on soil strength.

1

Preparation of Soil–Red Mud Mixtures

Soil was mixed with different percentages of red mud to prepare stabilized samples.

Mix Proportions Used

Sample Red Mud Content Description
S1 0% Natural Soil
S2 5% Red Mud Soil + 5% Red Mud
S3 10% Red Mud Soil + 10% Red Mud
S4 15% Red Mud Soil + 15% Red Mud
S5 20% Red Mud Soil + 20% Red Mud
2

Sample Preparation

The soil and red mud mixtures were prepared as follows:

✔ Mixed thoroughly to achieve uniform distribution of red mud in the soil
✔ Water was added as required to achieve desired consistency
✔ Samples were prepared in standard cylindrical moulds for UCS testing
3

UCS Testing of Stabilized Soil

The prepared samples were tested using the Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) testing machine to determine the compressive strength of soil stabilized with different percentages of red mud (0%, 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%).

4

Result Comparison

The UCS values of stabilized soil samples were compared with the UCS value of natural soil to:

✔ Analyze the strength improvement with increasing red mud content
✔ Identify the optimum percentage of red mud providing maximum strength

— Developed by Saurav Suman
